skill.name: workflow-patterns
skill.description: Guides agents through Conductor-style TDD task execution, phase checkpoints, git commits, verification gates, and plan updates.
skill.summary: Apply a structured TDD workflow with checkpoints, git notes, and verification gates.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found the content is markdown workflow guidance rather than executable code. The git and test command examples are legitimate for this skill, yet they can modify a repository, so the final risk is medium with publication allowed.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Modifying Workflow Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill provides markdown examples for git commits, git notes, and git revert operations. These commands match the skill purpose, but they can stage files, create commits, attach notes, or modify the working tree if followed without project review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in markdown examples and have clear repository side effects. The surrounding text presents them as workflow steps, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Tool Execution Guidance Requires User Context
medium_findings.description: The playbook tells agents to run test, coverage, lint, and type-checking commands. This is expected for a TDD workflow, but command execution should remain scoped to the active project and user-approved environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed commands are ordinary development tools with no network or obfuscation behavior. They still execute local tooling and may be unsafe in an untrusted repository.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Flags
low_findings.description: The static high-risk cryptography matches do not show weak hash or encryption code. The cited lines contain checklist items, markdown task markers, or ordinary workflow text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found no cryptographic API, algorithm selection, or secret handling. The scanner appears to have matched short tokens inside prose.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Flags
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance matches are normal workflow and verification examples. They do not instruct port scanning, host discovery, credential probing, or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are prose about tests, approval, form verification, and commit quality. No reconnaissance tooling, targets, or collection behavior is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains shell command examples for git, pytest, ruff, and mypy. These are legitimate development commands, but they should be executed only after confirming repository state and user intent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The static external-command pattern is real, but the commands appear inside documentation examples rather than executable scripts. The risk depends on an agent choosing to run them in a live repository.

content.value_statement: Software tasks often lose quality when tests, commits, and checkpoints are handled inconsistently. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a repeatable TDD workflow.
actual_capabilities: Guides task selection from a plan file in phase order.
Defines red, green, and refactor steps for implementation tasks.
Provides git commit, git note, and checkpoint patterns.
Lists verification gates for tests, coverage, linting, and type checks.
Explains how to document deviations, blockers, and approval checkpoints.
Gives task-type variations for models, APIs, integrations, and refactors.
limitations: Does not run tests or git commands by itself.
Assumes the project already uses plan files and checkpoint phases.
Does not provide domain-specific implementation code.
Requires human review before checkpoint approval.

use_cases.title: Implement Planned Tasks
use_cases.description: Follow a plan file from pending task selection through tests, implementation, commit, and status update.

use_cases.title: Manage Phase Checkpoints
use_cases.description: Create verification checklists, request approval, and record checkpoint commits after a phase is complete.

use_cases.title: Standardize Agent Workflows
use_cases.description: Give coding agents a predictable process for TDD, quality gates, and progress documentation.

prompt_templates.title: Start Next Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the workflow-patterns skill to select the next pending task from my plan and outline the TDD steps before editing.

prompt_templates.title: Verify Task Completion
prompt_templates.scenario: Check whether a task can be marked complete.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the workflow-patterns checklist to review this completed task for tests, coverage, documentation, and commit readiness.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Phase Checkpoint
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the workflow-patterns phase protocol to build a verification checklist and identify what needs approval before checkpointing.

prompt_templates.title: Recover From Workflow Drift
prompt_templates.scenario: Handle a deviation or blocked task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the workflow-patterns playbook to classify this deviation, update the plan, and propose the safest recovery sequence.

output_examples.input: Start the next task in the authentication plan.
output_examples.output: The agent identifies the next pending task, marks it in progress, writes expected failing tests, and lists the first verification commands.

output_examples.input: We finished phase one. What should happen before checkpointing?
output_examples.output: The agent summarizes changed files, required automated checks, manual verification items, and the approval request needed before a checkpoint commit.

output_examples.input: A dependency blocked the current task.
output_examples.output: The agent records the blocker, checks whether other tasks can proceed, and recommends a documented resolution path.

best_practices: Confirm repository state before running git commands.
Keep commits focused on one task or checkpoint.
Request explicit approval before phase checkpoint commits.
anti_patterns: Using broad git commands without reviewing changed files.
Marking tasks complete before verification gates pass.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance and command examples for an agent or user to apply deliberately.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: It is marked as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is this only for test driven development?
faq.answer: TDD is the main workflow, but the playbook also covers checkpoints, git notes, deviations, and recovery.

faq.question: Does it require Conductor?
faq.answer: It references Conductor-style tracks and plan files, so it works best in projects using that structure.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes git and local tooling examples that can modify a repository if used without review.

faq.question: Can it replace human approval?
faq.answer: No. The workflow explicitly requires human approval before phase checkpoint progression.
